content:
2 min read
Just now

--

Rainbows look magical, but the AI I asked to explain them refused to be poetic about it. It walked me through the physics like a detective laying out a case, and somehow that made them more magical, not less.
Step one: light isn’t actually one thing. Sunlight looks white, but it’s a smoothie of every color blended together. When it hits a raindrop, the light bends as it enters the water, because water is denser than air and slows light down. Different colors bend by slightly different amounts. Red bends the least, violet bends the most, and the rest fan out in between like a deck of cards.
Step two: the light hits the back of the raindrop and bounces. Then it bends again on the way out. By the time the colors leave the drop, they’ve been split into a tiny rainbow, with each color heading off at a slightly different angle. One drop, full spectrum, all the way out.
Step three, and this is where the AI got me, is that you don’t see one drop’s rainbow. You see millions. Each drop in the sky throws light in a specific direction, so for any given color, only the drops at exactly the right angle from your eye send that color to you. Red drops are higher, violet drops are lower, and the drops in between fill in everything else. Your eye stitches it all together into a perfect arc.
That’s also why every rainbow is personal. The angle depends on where you’re standing. The rainbow your friend sees is technically a different rainbow made of different drops. Run toward it and it moves with you, because the geometry follows your eyes.
You need sunlight, water, and an angle of roughly 42 degrees from the sun. Get those three, and reality politely paints you a color wheel. It’s not magic, it’s just light doing its taxes.
